claim_summary: The mechanism for microwave-induced SO2 and NOx oxidation is a microwave-induced catalytic reaction between SO2 and NOx and potassium permanganate with zeolite as the catalyst and microwave absorbent, producing sulfate and nitrates.

evidence_summary: The desulfurization and denitrification effect using potassium permanganate and zeolite together under microwave is much higher than using potassium permanganate or zeolite only.
The authors propose reaction equations showing KMnO4 reacting with NO and SO2 to form KNO3, MnO2, K2SO4, H2SO4, and O2, which further oxidize SO2 and NO to sulfate and nitrates.

claim_summary: The synergistic effect of microwave and catalyst treatment increases SO2 and NOx removal efficiency, with zeolite acting as a microwave absorbent and catalyst, and potassium permanganate as an oxidizing agent.

evidence_summary: Microwave addition to potassium permanganate and zeolite increases SO2 and NOx removal efficiency compared to without microwave.
The authors state that potassium permanganate does not absorb microwave energy and requires zeolite as a microwave absorbent to induce oxidation.

evidence_summary: Thermodynamic analysis shows that with CaO present, hydrogen purity exceeds 95% and CO concentration is substantially diminished.

title: Coke suppression of kerosene by wall catalytic steam reforming

transformation: kerosene + water → hydrogen + carbon monoxide + light hydrocarbons

method_details: Elemental nickel is added as an active component using calcium aluminate as the carrier using an impregnation method.
Catalyst is coated onto the inner wall of the reactor by plasma spraying, which is controlled to within 0.2mm.
Water and kerosene, together with 0.3% emulsifier, are fed into the fuel tank in advance and mixed to form an emulsion fuel using a gear pump and a static in-pipe mixer.

The in-pipe mixer contains static mixer elements and intermediate chambers. The static mixer element contains an 180° twisted blade. Owing to the multi-dimensional movement (horizontal, tangential and radial), the mixing of the emulsified fuel is further improved.
High purity nitrogen gas is used to remove residual fuel before and after every experiment.

claim_summary: Spraying a nickel-based catalyst on the reactor wall decreases coke formation by preventing the formation of filamentous coke on the metal surface.

evidence_summary: Without catalyst, the differential pressure increased to a maximum quickly, indicating channel restriction by coke, while with catalyst, the differential pressure remained low.

claim_summary: Catalytic steam reforming consumes coke precursors and produces hydrogen, which suppresses the polymerization of aromatics to polycyclic aromatics, thereby reducing coke formation.

evidence_summary: Adding water to the catalytic reaction increases hydrogen yield and decreases the proportion of aromatics and polycyclic aromatics in products.

claim_summary: Increasing water content in the feed promotes catalytic steam reforming, leading to higher hydrogen production that is consumed in hydrogenation reactions, suppressing olefin and aromatic formation and thus reducing coking rate.

evidence_summary: As water content increases, yields of hydrogen and carbon monoxide rise, while yields of ethylene, propylene, and aromatics decrease, correlating with lower coking rates.

claim_summary: Higher temperatures increase coking rate exponentially due to enhanced Diels-Alder and polymerization reactions that produce more polycyclic aromatics, which are coke precursors.

evidence_summary: Coking rate increases with temperature, and at temperatures above 680°C, the proportion of polycyclic aromatics in liquid products rises significantly.

claim_summary: Al is oxidized by supercritical ethanol to form various Al2O3 phases, which act as catalysts for ethanol reforming to produce additional hydrogen.

evidence_summary: XRD analysis shows that Al is partially oxidized by supercritical ethanol, forming η-, κ-, θ-, and α-Al2O3 phases in the Al-SCE system.
Gas composition analysis indicates hydrogen yield exceeds theoretical values from Al oxidation alone, suggesting ethanol reforming reactions occur.

claim_summary: In situ generated hydrogen from Al oxidation and ethanol reforming promotes hydrodeoxygenation, reducing oxygen content and increasing hydrocarbons in bio-crude oil.

evidence_summary: Elemental analysis shows decreased O/C and increased H/C ratios in bio-crude oil with higher Al loading, indicating enhanced deoxygenation.
GC-MS analysis reveals increased hydrocarbons and decreased acids and nitrogen compounds in bio-crude oil with Al addition, suggesting hydrogenation reactions.

claim_summary: An Al to RS ratio of 2:3 is optimal for maximizing bio-crude oil yield and energy recovery due to balanced hydrogen generation and ethanol solvent availability.

evidence_summary: Bio-crude oil yield and energy recovery peak at Al to RS ratio of 2:3, with higher ratios leading to decreased yield due to competition for ethanol.
Gas composition analysis shows hydrogen yield increases with Al loading, but excessive Al reduces oil yield by limiting ethanol availability for biomass degradation.

claim_summary: Hydrogen distribution along the catalyst bed reduces methane selectivity and enhances C5+ selectivity by lowering H2/CO ratio and increasing residence time at the bed inlet, favoring chain propagation over termination.

evidence_summary: Hydrogen distribution strategies (equal, increasing, decreasing) compared to co-feed show suppressed CH4 and C2–C4 selectivities and improved C5+ selectivity.
Under hydrogen distribution, H2/CO ratio is lower at the bed inlet and increases gradually, while residence time is higher at the beginning, opposing H2/CO changes.

claim_summary: Lower hydrogen concentration at the bed inlet reduces methane formation because higher hydrogen concentration favors methane-producing reactions.

evidence_summary: Methane selectivity decreases under hydrogen distribution strategies, with the largest reduction (about 20%) observed at longer residence times.

claim_summary: Hydrogen distribution increases olefin-to-paraffin ratio by lowering H2/CO ratio at the bed inlet, which favors olefin formation over hydrogenation.

evidence_summary: Olefin-to-paraffin ratio improves under hydrogen distribution strategies, with the most significant improvement in the increasing strategy where H2/CO ratio is lowest at the bed inlet.

claim_summary: At lower H2/CO ratios, chain propagation rates are favored over termination rates, leading to higher molecular weight hydrocarbon selectivity.

evidence_summary: Chain growth probability (α) increases under hydrogen distribution, consistent with higher C5+ selectivity and lower methane selectivity.

claim_summary: Xylose is first decomposed into intermediates including alcohols and aldehydes, which are then converted into ketones via homo- and cross-ketonization reactions over nano-CeO2.

claim_summary: The ketonization ability of CeO2 is attributed to its abundant surface basic sites that abstract α-H from reactants.

evidence_summary: Previous literature indicates that CeO2's surface basic sites initiate ketonization by abstracting α-H from carbonyl compounds.

claim_summary: Increasing CeO2 dosage promotes pathway 2, leading to higher yields of linear ketones like acetone, 2-butanone, and 3-pentanone, while inhibiting pathways 1 and 3 that produce furfural and formaldehyde.

evidence_summary: Higher CFRs increased peak areas of acetone and 2-butanone while decreasing furfural and hydroxy acetaldehyde.

claim_summary: Hydroxy acetaldehyde and furfural from xylose pyrolysis are mainly converted to mono-functional linear ketones, especially acetone and 2-butanone, over nano-CeO2.

evidence_summary: Catalytic pyrolysis with CeO2 reduced peak areas of hydroxy acetaldehyde and furfural while increasing acetone and 2-butanone.

claim_summary: Unburned carbon (UBC) in flyash catalyzes mercury oxidation by forming chlorinated-carbon sites that react with Hg0 to form HgCl, which desorbs as oxidized mercury.

evidence_summary: The authors propose a mechanism where UBC reacts with HCl to form chlorinated-carbon sites, which then react with Hg0 to form adsorbed HgCl that desorbs as oxidized mercury.
Coal blending experiments showed that increased UBC from bituminous coal enhanced mercury oxidation compared to chlorine injection alone.

claim_summary: Calcium and UBC synergistically enhance mercury capture, with UBC providing sites for Hg0 adsorption and oxidation, and calcium shifting the fraction to adsorbed HgCl for increased removal.

evidence_summary: Experiments with hydrated lime injection showed increased mercury removal only when UBC was present, indicating synergy between calcium and carbon.
The authors suggest calcium may prevent desorption of oxidized mercury or react with HgCl intermediate to enhance capture.

claim_summary: The type of carbon (e.g., from western vs. eastern bituminous coal) affects mercury removal and oxidation activity, with more active carbon having higher internal surface area.

evidence_summary: Western bituminous coal produced more active carbon for mercury removal and oxidation compared to eastern bituminous coals, attributed to higher internal surface area.

claim_summary: Mercury removal across an ESP is a function of carbon and calcium concentrations in the flue gas, with contour plots developed to describe their relationship.

evidence_summary: A quadratic model was derived from response-surface concepts to correlate mercury removal with carbon and calcium concentrations.
Data from coal blend tests were used to develop contour plots showing how carbon and calcium concentrations affect mercury removal.

claim_summary: The oxidative non-destructive mechanism of dibenzothiophene desulfurization, called the 4S pathway, is a prerogative of aerobic microorganisms like Pseudomonas sp., which can degrade a large number of compounds but may not be selective.

evidence_summary: The authors describe the oxidative non-destructive mechanism of DBT desulfurization as the 4S pathway, characteristic of aerobic microorganisms such as Pseudomonas sp., which can degrade many aliphatic, aromatic, and sulfur aromatic compounds.

claim_summary: The reductive mechanism of dibenzothiophene desulfurization is similar to conventional hydrodesulfurization and is performed by anaerobic microorganisms like Desulfovibrio desulfuricans, which do not highly degrade aliphatic and aromatic compounds.

evidence_summary: The authors state that the reductive mechanism holds interest for industrial applications as it is similar to conventional HDS and that sulfur-reducing bacteria like Desulfovibrio desulfuricans do not highly degrade aliphatic and aromatic compounds.

claim_summary: For Pseudomonas sp., the rate-limiting factors in dibenzothiophene biodegradation include microbial adherence to the hydrocarbons, diffusional mass transfer, and co-assimilation mechanisms, with DBT assimilation occurring at the interphase between organic and aqueous phases.

evidence_summary: The authors report that studies on DBT biodegradation by Pseudomonas sp. show that three concomitant factors are rate-limiting: microbial adherence to hydrocarbons, diffusional mass transfer, and co-assimilation mechanisms, with DBT assimilation located at the interphase.

claim_summary: Immobilization of Pseudomonas sp. on hydrophilic natural supports retains water around the catalyst, which is essential for biocatalytic function, and the hydrophobicity-hydrophilicity balance affects performance.

evidence_summary: The authors state that immobilization on hydrophilic supports retains water around the catalyst, essential for biocatalytic function, and the hydrophobicity-hydrophilicity balance is derived from polymer properties and water content.

claim_summary: Using a poorly biodegradable solvent like 2,2-4,4-6-8,8 heptamethylnonane with immobilized Pseudomonas sp. enables an ideal biodesulfurization process with obliged desulfurization selectivity despite the microorganism's low specificity.

evidence_summary: The authors describe that dissolving sulfur compounds in a poorly biodegradable solvent such as 2,2-4,4-6-8,8 heptamethylnonane allows studying an ideal biodesulfurization process with obliged desulfurization selectivity using non-selective Pseudomonas sp.

Heat produced at Pt sites by TOX can be transferred to Ni sites to provide heat for endothermic SR, even at atomic distance due to Pt-Ni surface alloy formation.
Autothermal conditions were achieved in performance tests with limited heat input, indicating efficient heat transfer between sites.

claim_summary: Increasing OSR reaction temperature suppresses methanation reactions, leading to higher H2 and lower CH4 production.

evidence_summary: Higher temperatures favor higher hydrogen and lower methane production due to the endothermicity of steam reforming and exothermicity of methanation reactions.

evidence_summary: SEM-EDX mapping shows well-dispersed Au and Re particles on the freshly reduced catalyst.

claim_summary: Increasing oxygen concentration in the feed enhances total oxidation, leading to higher CO2 and CO production and suppressed methanation.

evidence_summary: Increasing O2 concentration increased steady-state CO2 and CO concentrations while decreasing CH4 concentration, indicating enhanced TOX and suppressed methanation.

claim_summary: The deactivation of the Ni/Mg-Al-O/AC bifunctional catalyst in the aldol condensation-hydrogenation of cyclopentanone is caused by water formed in the reaction, which leads to the formation of a compound (composed of Al, O, and H) that covers Ni species and reduces hydrogenation activity.

The use of n-pentane as an organic solvent improves catalyst reusability by isolating water from the catalyst, as shown by stable conversion and yield over multiple runs compared to solvent-free conditions.

claim_summary: The selectivity towards targeted alkanes in the aldol condensation-hydrogenation of cyclopentanone is influenced by solvent polarity, with non-polar n-pentane providing the highest yield by reducing water-catalyst contact and minimizing side reactions.

evidence_summary: Screening tests with solvents of different polarities (n-pentane, THF, DMSO) show that n-pentane gives the highest yield of targeted alkanes, while higher polarity solvents lead to lower yields and increased intermediate selectivity.

claim_summary: In the one-step synchronous condensation-hydrogenation at 260°C, cyclopentanone is directly hydrogenated to cyclopentanol as a side reaction, reducing the yield of targeted alkanes.

evidence_summary: Real-time tracking shows cyclopentanol yield increases continuously to about 15.2% in the one-step reaction, indicating direct hydrogenation of cyclopentanone occurs before self-condensation.

claim_summary: The low-temperature condensation and high-temperature hydrogenation scheme avoids direct hydrogenation of cyclopentanone by performing condensation at 170°C (where hydrogenation does not occur) followed by hydrogenation at 260°C, improving yield and reducing energy consumption.

evidence_summary: In the one-pot scheme, cyclopentanol is not detected during the first 6 h at 170°C, confirming direct hydrogenation is prevented at lower temperatures.
The one-pot scheme yields higher targeted alkane production (70.7%) compared to the one-step scheme (64.4%), with reduced cyclopentanol formation.

claim_summary: The synergetic catalytic system of NaOH and noble metal catalysts (Ru/C, Pt/C, Pd/C) facilitates lignin depolymerization and reduces char formation under mild conditions by cleaving ether bonds and hydrogenating unstable intermediates.

evidence_summary: The activation energy of depolymerization reactions is lower than that of repolymerization reactions, favoring depolymerization at lower temperatures.
2D-HSQC NMR and 31P NMR analysis showed that S-type and G-type units were degraded, and p-hydroxyphenyl units and cyclohexanols increased, indicating demethoxylation and hydrogenation.
Comparison with non-catalyst runs showed that noble metal catalysts significantly increased yields of oil-soluble and water-soluble products and reduced char formation.

claim_summary: The surface of Ru/C becomes more porous and eroded during depolymerization, enhancing lignin degradation.

evidence_summary: SEM images showed that recovered Ru/C had a more loose and porous surface compared to fresh Ru/C, indicating erosion of the catalyst support.

claim_summary: Noble metal catalysts enhance hydrogenation, converting lignin intermediates into aliphatic compounds and reducing repolymerization.

evidence_summary: 31P NMR analysis detected cyclohexanols in degraded lignin with Ru/C, indicating hydrogenation of aromatic units.
GC-MS analysis showed increased phenolic compounds and aromatic hydrocarbons with noble metal catalysts compared to non-catalyst runs.

claim_summary: NaOH cleaves ether bonds in lignin, but excess NaOH promotes repolymerization, affecting product yields.

evidence_summary: Excess NaOH catalyzes both depolymerization and repolymerization reactions, leading to decreased oil-soluble product yields.
Varying NaOH dosage showed that oil-soluble product yield decreased with excess NaOH, while char increased.

claim_summary: S-type units in lignin are more susceptible to demethoxylation than G-type units during catalytic depolymerization.

evidence_summary: 2D-HSQC NMR analysis showed that S-type units disappeared and G-type units decreased, with p-hydroxyphenyl units appearing.
31P NMR analysis indicated that S- and G-type phenolic OH groups were hardly observed, while p-hydroxyphenyl OH increased.

claim_summary: CH4 and CO play a synergistic role in the CO/CH4 mixture gas decomposition process, with H2 as a medium to promote the decomposing reactions further for each other.

evidence_summary: TG results show that carbon weight gain increases under low temperature for the mixture gas of CO/CH4 compared to pure CH4 decomposition, and the final carbon weight gain increases with CO partial pressure enhancement.

In the fluidized bed, X_CH4, X_CO, Y_H2, and carbon weight gain are increased significantly in the mixture gas of CO/CH4 compared to pure CO and CH4 gas decomposition processes, with carbon weight gain more than five times higher at 550°C.
The presence of H2 from CH4 decomposition promotes CO conversion and carbon weight gain in CO/H2 gas, indicating H2 acts as a medium in the mixture gas system.

claim_summary: The addition of CO lowers the initial carbon deposition temperature and shifts the maximum carbon deposition rate to lower temperatures in the CO/CH4 mixture gas decomposition.

evidence_summary: TG results show that with CO addition, the temperature of initial carbon weight gain decreases from 425°C to 350°C, and the temperature of maximum carbon deposition rate decreases from 575°C to 450°C.

claim_summary: High temperature in CH4 decomposition causes rapid carbon deposition that covers the catalyst surface, leading to deactivation and decline in CH4 conversion.

evidence_summary: At 650°C, CH4 conversion decreases to 28% after 28 min due to rapid carbon deposition covering the catalyst surface.
Carbon weight gain is lower at 650°C than at 550°C because carbon deposits quickly and impedes catalytic activity.

claim_summary: CO disproportionation is an exothermic reaction with thermodynamic limitations at high temperatures, leading to decreased conversion and carbon weight gain above 550°C.

evidence_summary: CO conversion and carbon weight gain decrease with increasing temperature, with values at 650°C being just 1/5 of those at lower temperatures.

claim_summary: The addition of CO to CH4 improves the length and graphitization of carbon nanotubes produced, with smoother and more even walls at higher CO/CH4 ratios.

evidence_summary: SEM and TEM images show that carbon products from CO/CH4 mixture gas are longer and smoother compared to those from pure CH4 or CO decomposition.
Raman spectroscopy indicates that the ID/IG ratio increases with higher CO/CH4 ratios, indicating enhanced graphitization of carbon nanotubes.

claim_summary: The deactivation of the Ni/YSZ anode in SOFCs fueled by biodiesel formulations is caused by carbon deposition from cracking of biodiesel, which produces ethylene and aromatics that lead to coke formation and pore blockage.

evidence_summary: When pure biodiesel was used, the cell deactivated within seconds due to cracking producing ethylene and aromatics, and lack of steam for reforming reactions.
The authors state that cracking of biodiesel produces ethylene and aromatics, which are intermediates in coke formation via polymerization and condensation reactions.

claim_summary: Increase in triglyceride content in biodiesel formulations leads to higher carbon deposition and deactivation due to increased formation of aromatics and ethylene.

evidence_summary: The authors state that higher triglyceride content increases carbon deposition by promoting aromatics and ethylene formation.
Butyl ester formulation, which had higher triglyceride content, showed worst performance and required air for stable operation, indicating more carbon deposition.

claim_summary: The performance of biodiesel formulations in SOFCs depends on the chain length of the alcohol used in ester preparation, with shorter chains (methyl) giving better power density than longer chains (ethyl, butyl).

evidence_summary: The authors state that with increase in chain length of alcohols, cell performance decreases, attributing it to higher carbon deposition tendencies.

claim_summary: Addition of water (steam) to biodiesel formulations improves performance and longevity by increasing hydrogen production via steam reforming and aiding carbon removal from the anode surface.

evidence_summary: 20% formulations with higher water content lasted longer than 50% formulations due to increased steam/carbon ratio enhancing carbon removal.
The authors state that steam reforming of biodiesel increases hydrogen production and helps remove carbon from the anode surface.

claim_summary: The use of emulsifiers in some biodiesel formulations can contribute to carbon deposition and deactivation, as their cracking adds to coke formation.

evidence_summary: Ethyl ester formulations, which did not require emulsifiers, performed better and lasted longer, while methyl and butyl formulations with emulsifiers had shorter lifetimes.
The authors state that cracking of emulsifier can be one of the reasons for coke deposition.

claim_summary: Introducing USY zeolite into the γ-Al2O3 support reduces metal-support interactions and favors formation of the NiMoS active phase, enhancing hydrodesulfurization activity.

evidence_summary: H2 TPR shows that the high temperature reduction peak for the composite-supported catalyst is weaker and shifted to lower temperatures compared to pure Al2O3-supported catalyst.
Modeling results indicate that increasing zeolite content in the support decreases sulfur content of the hydrotreated gasoil, correlating with higher catalyst activity.

claim_summary: Using EDTA as a chelating agent during catalyst synthesis weakens metal-support interactions and improves distribution of active sites, leading to enhanced HDS activity.

evidence_summary: H2 TPR shows that reduction peaks for the catalyst prepared with EDTA are shifted to lower temperatures compared to the catalyst without EDTA.
Modeling results show that increasing EDTA/Ni molar ratio reduces sulfur content in the hydrotreated gasoil, indicating improved catalyst performance.

claim_summary: The acidic properties of the USY zeolite in the composite support increase catalyst activity in the hydrogenation pathway for hydrodesulfurization of heavy sulfur species.

evidence_summary: NH3-TPD shows that increasing zeolite content in the composite support increases desorbed NH3 and acidity.
Modeling results indicate that higher zeolite content leads to lower sulfur content in the hydrotreated gasoil, supporting enhanced activity through hydrogenation.

claim_summary: Catalysts with average pore sizes in the range of 88 to 107 Å have favorable textural properties for achieving high activity in hydrodesulfurization of the gasoil blend.

evidence_summary: Nitrogen adsorption-desorption shows that catalyst supports have average pore sizes in the range of 88 to 107 Å, which are considered optimal based on previous studies.
Modeling and experimental results show high HDS activity for catalysts with these pore sizes, indicating no diffusion limitations.

claim_summary: The optimum catalyst synthesis conditions include 10 wt% USY zeolite in the support, 18 wt% total metal loading, Ni/(Ni+Mo)=0.417, and EDTA/Ni=2, which maximize hydrodesulfurization activity.

evidence_summary: Response surface modeling identified these conditions as optimal for minimizing sulfur content in the hydrotreated gasoil blend.
Validation experiment with the optimum catalyst showed sulfur content of 3.1 ppm in the hydrotreated gasoil, confirming model predictions.

Tar yields increase with increasing rank of parent materials used for char catalysts, showing lower activity for high-rank chars.

evidence_summary: In pyrolysis experiments, tar consists primarily of 2–4 fused aromatic rings, with more significant changes in 3–4 fused rings than 2 fused ones.

claim_summary: In the presence of steam, transformation of small aromatics to large ones occurs, facilitated by H radicals.

evidence_summary: Gasification experiments show tar contains larger aromatic compounds, including discernible 5 fused rings, compared to pyrolysis.
The presence of 5 fused aromatic rings supports speculation of transforming/condensation reactions facilitated by H radicals from steam.

claim_summary: Char catalysts from low-rank fuels crack unstable structures (aliphatic and O-containing) without steam, while steam preferentially reforms unstable tar components in blank and graphite char cases.

evidence_summary: In blank and graphite char cases, steam reduces tar yields, while with low-rank char catalysts, steam does not change tar yields.
Chars from low-rank fuels contain abundant functional groups and disordered carbon structure that can crack unstable tar structures regardless of steam.

claim_summary: Coke formation on the catalyst involves initial fast adsorption of heavy components from the atmospheric residue onto the catalyst, followed by condensation reactions that generate coke with increasing radical concentration over time.

evidence_summary: The radical concentration of coke on the catalyst increases over time and is faster at higher temperatures, indicating condensation of coke structure.
The coking rate on the catalyst increases linearly with time after an initial fast period, following zero-order kinetics with activation energy determined from Arrhenius plots.

In the absence of THN, coke yield and radical concentration show similar trends over time, except in the initial 3 minutes where coke yield increases quickly while radical concentration decreases slightly.

claim_summary: Hydrogen donation from tetrahydronaphthalene (THN) reduces coking rate and activation energy by inhibiting condensation reactions through hydrogenation of radical fragments.

evidence_summary: The apparent activation energy for coking is lower in the presence of THN compared to its absence, indicating reduced coking rate increase with temperature due to hydrogen donation.
The radical concentration of coke on the catalyst is much lower in the presence of THN, and the H/C ratio of coke is higher with THN, showing less condensation.

THN conversion data show that hydrogen donation occurs, and in the presence of THN, radical concentration in the sample declines over time at lower temperatures, indicating hydrogenation of radical fragments.

claim_summary: The reaction of atmospheric residue involves cracking to generate radical fragments, which then undergo coupling and condensation to form heavy products like coke, with radical concentration as a sensitive indicator.

evidence_summary: Radical concentration increases over time in the absence of THN, showing formation of heavy products that confine unpaired electrons detectable by ESR.
The activation energy for increase in radical concentration is determined from Arrhenius equation, indicating temperature-dependent cracking and condensation.

claim_summary: The carbon source for CNTs growth during K-catalyzed pyrolysis of coal/coke is the aromatic CC, CH, and ether bond C-O-C structures in the solid coal/coke, not the gas-phase carbon species.

evidence_summary: FTIR analysis shows that the absorption peak intensities of aromatic CC, CH, and C-O-C groups decrease with residence time in RC-K and CC-K pyrolysis products, indicating these structures are consumed during CNTs formation.
Gas chromatography shows that carbon-containing gases like CH4, C2H6, and C3H8 are no longer generated before reaching 900°C, suggesting gas-phase carbon sources have limited effect on CNTs growth.

Raman deconvolution shows that ID3/IG and ID4/IG ratios, representing amorphous carbon and ether bond structures, have a negative linear correlation with CNTs content, indicating these carbon structures are the main carbon source.

claim_summary: KOH addition catalyzes CNTs formation by converting to K-containing species (K2CO3, K2O, K) that etch the carbon structure and promote reduction to metal K, which then reacts with ether bond structures to release carbon atoms for CNTs growth.

evidence_summary: SEM analysis shows CNTs are present in RC-K and CC-K pyrolysis products but absent in RC and CC products, indicating KOH addition is directly related to CNTs formation.
XRD analysis shows graphite (002) diffraction peaks appear and intensify with residence time in RC-K and CC-K products, correlating with increased CNTs content due to K-catalyzed transformation.
Raman analysis shows IG/ID values increase significantly with residence time in RC-K products, indicating enhanced graphitization due to CNTs formation catalyzed by K components.

claim_summary: The probable growth mechanism involves aromatic CC and CH reducing K components to metal K, which then converts ether bond C-O-C structures into carbon atoms/clusters that form CNTs on Fe catalyst particles derived from primary siderite in coal.

evidence_summary: The authors propose a reaction pathway based on previous studies and their results, where aromatic CC and CH reduce K2CO3/K2O to metal K, which reacts with C-O-C to release carbon atoms that form CNTs via Fe catalyst.

Previous studies cited show that using graphite instead of coal with KOH catalytic pyrolysis results in etching but no CNTs, indicating aromatic CC alone cannot directly form CNTs and supports the role of ether bond structures.

evidence_summary: Kinetic modeling results show that activation energy increases with alcohol chain length, and methane activation energy is higher than those of alcohols, consistent with literature trends.

claim_summary: The kinetic model developed at lab scale can predict catalyst performance at a larger bench scale, indicating that intrinsic kinetics are captured without overfitting.

evidence_summary: Validation experiments at bench scale showed that the model predicted product flows reasonably well, confirming its predictive capability for larger scales.

claim_summary: A kinetic model can reasonably predict the effect of methanol co-feeding even if its parameters are determined without methanol co-feeding experiments, though with increased uncertainty.

evidence_summary: Fitting parameters with and without methanol co-feeding experiments showed significant variation, but the model still provided acceptable predictions for methanol co-feeding effects.

claim_summary: A suitable ratio of Lewis acid to Brönsted acid and their synergistic effects are critical for achieving high aromatization activity and stability in FCC gasoline upgrading.

evidence_summary: IR of chemisorbed pyridine shows that the total Lewis/Brönsted acidity and the medium and strong Lewis/Brönsted acidity of the catalysts increase notably after modification, with a total Lewis to Brönsted acid ratio of 2.3 for Catalyst C.
Catalyst C showed a high aromatization activity and the best stability among the modified catalysts, with aromatic content increasing from 11.4 to 29.1 vol.%.

claim_summary: The most suitable acidity with a Lewis/Brönsted acid sites ratio of 2.3, resulting from combined-modification, leads to excellent aromatization activity, isomerization activity, and high ability of reducing olefin content without RON loss.

evidence_summary: NH3-TPD and IR results show that the acid strength of the parent nanoscale HZSM-5 is weakened and strong acid sites are decreased significantly, with Catalyst C having a Lewis/Brönsted acid sites ratio of 2.3.

Catalyst C had excellent aromatization activity and isomerization activity to reduce olefin content in FCC gasoline, with olefin content lowered from 49.6 to 8.1 vol.% and i-paraffins/n-paraffins ratio increased from 5.1 to 6.7–7.0.

claim_summary: Decreasing strong acid sites by hydrothermal treatment above 400 °C prevents coke deposition and improves catalytic stability in olefin reduction.

evidence_summary: NH3-TPD shows that strong acid sites on the modified catalysts decreased drastically when steaming above 400 °C.
Catalyst C had the best stability in olefin reduction, while Catalyst B with high acidity showed poor stability with olefin content increasing drastically with time on stream.

claim_summary: Loading with lanthanum and zinc oxides improves the catalyst aromatization activity, isomerization activity, and catalytic stability.

evidence_summary: Modification of parent nanoscale HZSM-5 by hydrothermal treatment and impregnation with La and Zn oxides leads to improved catalytic performance compared to the parent catalyst.

claim_summary: The CuCo solid solution with coexistence of Cu0 and Cu+ species, along with lattice Co atoms, is the active site for stearic acid deoxygenation, where a high ratio of Cu0/(Cu++Cu0) and lattice Co atoms leads to high catalytic activity.

evidence_summary: XRD shows a slight shift to lower diffraction angle for CuCo-R compared to Cu-R, indicating Co doping into Cu lattice forming a solid solution.
XPS and Cu LMM spectra confirm the coexistence of Cu+ and Cu0 on the surface of CuCo-R catalyst.
Theoretical calculation shows CuCo-R catalyst has higher energy differences for reaction paths producing long-chain alkanes compared to Cu-R, indicating enhanced activity.
Comparison of CuCo-C catalyst at different reaction times shows that higher Cu0/(Cu++Cu0) ratio and more lattice Co atoms correlate with higher stearic acid conversion.

claim_summary: The deoxygenation of stearic acid over CuCo-R proceeds via initial hydrogenation to octadecanal, which then undergoes decarbonylation to n-heptadecane (major route) or hydrogenation to octadecanol followed by dehydration-hydrogenation to n-octadecane (minor route).

evidence_summary: Time-dependent experiments show octadecanal as an intermediate with limited detection, and octadecanol content declines as n-heptadecane selectivity increases.
Product distribution analysis indicates n-heptadecane is the major product, with equilibrium between octadecanal and octadecanol affecting selectivity.

claim_summary: Doping Co into Cu to form CuCo solid solution inhibits cracking reactions and promotes long-chain alkane production by reducing acid sites and enhancing interaction between Cu and Co.

evidence_summary: NH3-TPD shows CuCo-R has weaker acidity compared to Cu-R, which reduces cracking.
CuCo-R exhibits much lower cracking product selectivity compared to Cu-R and Co-R.
Theoretical calculation shows CuCo-R has a negative energy difference for cracking reaction path, indicating inhibition, while Cu-R has a higher positive value.

claim_summary: The strong interaction between Cu and Co in the CuCo solid solution, facilitated by CNT support, enhances catalytic activity by promoting reduction, improving dispersion, and increasing exposed surface area.

evidence_summary: H2-TPR shows reduction peaks shift to lower temperature for CuCo-C compared to CuC, indicating easier reduction due to Co addition.
TEM shows CuCo-R has smaller particle size and better dispersion compared to Cu-R, attributed to Co doping.
CuCo-R achieves higher conversion and selectivity to long-chain alkanes compared to monometallic catalysts.

claim_summary: The calcined CuCo-C catalyst undergoes rapid in-situ reduction under reaction conditions to form metallic Cu and CoO, with catalytic performance comparable to pre-reduced CuCo-R.

evidence_summary: XRD of used CuCo-C catalyst shows metallic Cu phases and no Co/Co oxide peaks, indicating in-situ reduction.
CuCo-C catalyst at 240 min reaction time shows high conversion and product distribution similar to CuCo-R.

evidence_summary: FT-ICR MS analysis shows pseudo-conversion HDS rate orders for sulfur families at different HDS rates.
HDS kinetic analysis based on DBE lumping reveals reactivity trends with hydrotreating severity.

claim_summary: The most refractory sulfur compounds during deep RHT process have DBE range of 10–12, followed by 9, and carbon number range of 36–38.

evidence_summary: FT-ICR MS analysis identifies DBE and carbon number ranges of S1 class compounds in deeply-hydrogenated residues.
Pseudo-conversion rates show S1 class with DBE=10–12 have lowest reactivity in most severely-hydrotreated sample.

claim_summary: The skeletons of the most refractory sulfur compounds mainly consist of 2–3 aromatic rings and 0–1 naphthenic/cyclohexene ring, with alkyl side chains (C4–9) near sulfur atom positions.

evidence_summary: CID FT-ICR MS analysis indicates fragment ions from refractory compounds have unchanged or slightly reduced DBE, suggesting aromatic-dominated skeletons.
Carbon number distribution of fragment ions shows alkyl side chains with carbon numbers of 4–9, possibly at positions near sulfur atom.

claim_summary: During deep RHT process, the hydrogenation (HYD) route plays a more important role than direct desulfurization (DDS) due to high steric hindrance of sulfur compounds.

evidence_summary: Analysis suggests HYD route is favored for multi-aromatic-ring thiophenes under severe conditions due to increased steric hindrance.

claim_summary: Under oxygen presence, Ni catalysts exist as oxidized Ni2+ species which are active for methane combustion but inactive for reforming, leading to separated exothermic and endothermic reaction zones in the catalyst bed.

evidence_summary: The temperature profile of G-91 shows a high bed temperature at the inlet, indicating combustion occurs there, with reforming only after oxygen is consumed.
When quartz sand is used instead of catalysts, no methane combustion or temperature increase is observed, confirming the catalytic role.

claim_summary: Pt catalysts maintain metallic Pt0 state under oxygen presence, allowing simultaneous methane combustion and reforming on the same catalyst surface, leading to overlapping reaction zones and a flat temperature profile.

evidence_summary: The temperature profile of Pt(10)/Al2O3 shows a flat bed temperature with the inlet temperature similar to the wall temperature, indicating overlapping combustion and reforming zones.
Comparison with Ni catalysts shows Pt catalysts have lower oxygen affinity, maintaining metallic state and enabling simultaneous reactions.

claim_summary: High Pt loading (e.g., Pt(10)/Al2O3 and Pt(1)/Al2O3) is necessary to achieve high reforming activity and balance exothermic and endothermic heats, leading to efficient heat transfer and flat temperature profiles.

evidence_summary: Pt catalysts with higher loading (Pt(10)/Al2O3 and Pt(1)/Al2O3) show flat temperature profiles and high conversion, while lower loadings (Pt(0.3)/Al2O3 and Pt(0.1)/Al2O3) have high inlet temperatures and lower activity.

claim_summary: Ni catalysts with lower reforming activity result in longer distances between exothermic and endothermic zones, causing higher bed temperatures and less efficient heat transfer.

evidence_summary: Ni(10)/Al2O3 and Ni(30)/Al2O3 show higher bed temperatures and lower reforming activity compared to G-91, indicating separated reaction zones.

claim_summary: The overlap of combustion and reforming zones on high-loading Pt catalysts leads to efficient heat transfer and balanced reaction heats, reducing hot spots and improving energy efficiency.

evidence_summary: Pt(10)/Al2O3 and Pt(1)/Al2O3 show drastic decreases in bed temperature with addition of reforming gas, indicating balanced exothermic and endothermic reactions.

claim_summary: Both HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 promote the removal of non-phenolic hydroxyl functional groups from guaiacol, reducing selectivity of products like salicylaldehyde, o-cresol, and o-ethylphenol.

DFT calculations show energy barriers for catechol generation from guaiacol over HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 are lower than the bond dissociation energy for methyl removal in pyrolysis alone.
Experimental results show reduced selectivity of salicylaldehyde, o-cresol, and o-ethylphenol in catalytic cracking of guaiacol over HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 compared to pyrolysis alone.

evidence_summary: Py-IR characterization shows Zn/HZSM-5 has increased Lewis acid sites compared to HZSM-5.

claim_summary: Both HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 promote the conversion of acetic acid to vinyl ketone, with the decomposition of propionyl cations as the main pathway for ethylene and CO generation.

evidence_summary: DFT calculations show that the energy barrier for intramolecular hydrogen transfer of acetic acid to 1,1-ethanediol is reduced over HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 compared to pyrolysis alone.
DFT calculations indicate that the decomposition of propionyl cations has lower energy barriers than acetyl cation decomposition, identifying it as the main pathway for CO and ethylene formation.
Experimental results show increased CO and CO2 yields and changes in ethylene/butene volume percentages when using HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 for acetic acid catalytic cracking compared to pyrolysis alone.

claim_summary: Zn/HZSM-5 is more effective than HZSM-5 in promoting intramolecular hydrogen transfer in acetic acid conversion, with lower energy barriers for key steps.

evidence_summary: DFT calculations show that energy barriers for the formation of 1,1-ethanediol and dehydration to vinyl ketone over Zn/HZSM-5 are lower than those over HZSM-5.
Experimental results show complete decomposition of acetic acid and higher CO/CO2 yields with Zn/HZSM-5 compared to HZSM-5.

claim_summary: Catechol is more likely to generate olefins and CO via ring-opening reactions rather than direct removal of phenolic hydroxyl groups during guaiacol catalytic cracking.

evidence_summary: DFT calculations show that energy barriers for catechol ring-opening to produce 1,3-butadiene and CO are much lower than those for direct removal of phenolic hydroxyl groups.

claim_summary: The use of zeolite catalysts changes the rate-determining step in acetic acid conversion to the dehydration of 1,1-ethanediol to vinyl ketone.

evidence_summary: DFT calculations show that the energy barrier for dehydration of 1,1-ethanediol to vinyl ketone is higher than for its formation, making dehydration the rate-determining step over HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5.

claim_summary: The highest catalytic activity of Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 in DRM is due to more active metallic nickel particles available on the catalyst's surface, which are stabilized by strong interaction with the NiNd2O4 support.

evidence_summary: Hydrogen chemisorption showed that Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 have high surface nickel amounts (36 and 24 μmol/g-cat., respectively).
CH4 conversion at 750°C followed the trend Ni5Nd5 > Ni7Nd3 > Ni3Nd7 > Ni1Nd9 > Ni9Nd1, which parallels the surface nickel amounts.
TPR profiles indicated strong interaction between Ni2+ and Nd2O3 in Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3, with reduction peaks above 500°C.

claim_summary: Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 exhibit good carbon-resistance ability due to effective mesopore size and larger surface area, which accommodate deposited carbon without clogging pore structure.

evidence_summary: Nitrogen physisorption showed Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 have effective mesopore sizes (40.3 and 18.2 nm, respectively) and larger surface areas (15 and 29 m²/g).
TG-DTA analysis indicated less graphitic carbon on spent Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3, with low-intensity exothermic peaks above 430°C.
Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 showed remarkable stability during 24h DRM reaction with minimal deactivation.

claim_summary: Deactivation of Ni1Nd9, Ni3Nd7, and Ni9Nd1 is caused by factors such as carbon deposition blocking pores, weak metal-support interaction, and coverage of Ni species by Nd2O3.

evidence_summary: TG-DTA analysis showed carbon deposition on spent Ni1Nd9, Ni3Nd7, and Ni9Nd1, with Ni9Nd1 having the highest amount (7.01e-2 g C/g-catalyst).
Nitrogen physisorption of spent catalysts indicated severe reduction in surface area for Ni1Nd9 and Ni3Nd7 due to carbon deposition and pore blockage.
TPR profiles showed weak interaction between Ni and Nd2O3 in Ni1Nd9 and Ni3Nd7, with lower reduction temperatures.
Ni1Nd9 and Ni3Nd7 exhibited severe or evident deactivation during stability tests.

claim_summary: During DRM reaction, Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 undergo reconstruction with segregation of metallic nickel from NiNd2O4 phase, providing more active sites and enhancing stability.

evidence_summary: TEM of spent Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 showed the presence of both NiNd2O4 and metallic nickel phases, indicating nickel segregation during reaction.
Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 displayed stable CH4 and CO2 conversions over 24h, attributed to the segregated nickel providing continuous active sites.

claim_summary: The control of Ni/Nd molar ratio in the range of 5/5 to 7/3 leads to predominant formation of NiNd2O4, which along with metallic nickel, optimizes catalyst performance by balancing active sites and support interaction.

evidence_summary: XRD and TEM analysis indicated that Ni/Nd molar ratio in the range of 3/7 to 7/3 results in predominant NiNd2O4 formation, while outside this range, Nd2O3 or metallic nickel dominates.
Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 showed the highest activity and stability among all catalysts, correlating with the optimal Ni/Nd ratio.

Different apparent activation energies are observed for ethanol steam reforming over Ni/biochar, biochar, and acid-washed biochar, indicating distinct reaction mechanisms.
Acid-washed biochar shows lower conversion and H2 yield compared to biochar, indicating AAEM species contribute to catalytic activity.

claim_summary: Low CO2 formation during ethanol steam reforming over Ni/biochar is due to hindered water-gas shift activity caused by preferential adsorption of ethanol-derived species on biochar sites and a H-rich surface.

evidence_summary: CO/CO2 ratios remain above 1 during ethanol steam reforming over Ni/biochar, unlike typical Ni catalysts where ratios approach equilibrium, indicating altered reaction mechanism.
Independent water-gas shift experiments over Ni/biochar show high CO conversion, indicating WGS is not inherently inhibited but hindered during ethanol steam reforming.

Raman spectroscopy shows similar depletion of O-containing functional groups during ethanol steam reforming over Ni/biochar and biochar alone, suggesting ethanol preferentially adsorbs on biochar sites.

claim_summary: Ethanol decomposition on biochar proceeds via a pathway involving OH cleavage to form ethyl surface species, leading to ethane formation instead of methane.

evidence_summary: Ethanol decomposition over biochar produces C2H6 as a dominant product, which is not commonly observed in literature, suggesting a unique reaction pathway.

claim_summary: The rate-limiting step for ethanol steam reforming and decomposition occurs on biochar sites within the dehydrogenation pathway, as indicated by kinetic compensation effect.

evidence_summary: Kinetic compensation effect is observed with a linear relationship between activation energies and pre-exponential factors across different catalysts, suggesting a common rate-limiting step on biochar.

claim_summary: The presence of steam during ethanol steam reforming helps maintain the population of O-containing functional groups on biochar, enhancing catalytic activity.

evidence_summary: Raman spectroscopy shows that total Raman peak area reduction is lower with increasing steam partial pressure, indicating steam facilitates replenishment of O-containing functional groups.
H2 yield and CO2 selectivity increase with higher steam partial pressure, correlating with maintained O-containing functional groups.

claim_summary: The reactions on Ni and biochar active sites are not independent; interactions between Ni and biochar sites affect the overall reaction mechanism.

evidence_summary: Subtracting the effect of biochar from Ni/biochar data does not match trends observed over Ni on inert supports, indicating non-independent site interactions.

claim_summary: Partial dealloying of FeNi3 alloy during DRM, with Fe species migrating to the surface and oxidizing, is the main factor contributing to deactivation of FeNiAl catalysts.

evidence_summary: XRD patterns of spent FeNiAl catalysts show decreased intensity and shift to higher angles for FeNi3 alloy peaks, indicating dealloying.
STEM-EDX analysis of spent 0.7-FeNiAl shows isolation of Ni and Fe particles, with Fe individually dispersed, unlike the alloy phase in reduced catalyst.
XPS analysis shows surface Fe species increased and Ni decreased in spent catalysts, with Fe0 absent and Fe2+/Fe3+ increased, indicating oxidation and migration.

evidence_summary: TEM images show ordered mesoporous structures with metal particles embedded in the walls, and spent catalysts retain well-preserved morphology with only slight particle size increase.

Carbon deposition rates are low (<1 mg-C/g-cat·h) for x-FeNiAl catalysts after 24 h reaction, indicating effective coke resistance.

evidence_summary: TGA analysis shows 0.7-FeNiAl has the lowest total carbon formation, with minimal amorphous carbon compared to other FeNiAl catalysts.

claim_summary: The catalytic activity of MoOx/nanorod-OMS-2 catalysts depends on the strong interaction between molybdenum oxide and the OMS-2 support, which creates strong acidic sites and enhances dispersion of molybdenum oxide species.

evidence_summary: H2-TPR analysis shows that with increasing molybdenum loading, reduction peaks shift to higher temperatures, indicating stronger interaction between support and molybdenum oxide.
NH3-TPD analysis shows that with increasing molybdenum loading, the intensity and maximum temperature of acidic peaks increase, indicating an increase in amount and strength of acidic sites.
SEM and TEM images show spherical molybdenum oxide particles dispersed on the nanorod surface, with dispersion decreasing at higher loadings.
Catalytic activity tests show that Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 has the highest conversion, correlating with optimal dispersion and strong acidic sites.

claim_summary: The oxidation of DBT proceeds via formation of metal peroxide intermediates (manganese hydroperoxide and hydro peroxymolybdate) from H2O2, followed by nucleophilic attack of sulfur on peroxo species to produce sulfoxide and sulfone.

evidence_summary: The authors propose a mechanism based on literature and characterization results, involving nucleophilic attack and redox cycles of manganese and molybdenum species.
Kinetic studies show pseudo-first-order dependence on DBT with an apparent activation energy of 18.9 kJ mol−1, consistent with the proposed oxidation pathway.

claim_summary: The Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst is stable and recyclable with minimal leaching of Mo or Mn species during the liquid-phase reaction.

evidence_summary: Leaching tests show no significant catalytic activity in the filtrate after catalyst removal, indicating no leaching of active species.
XRD and FT-IR analysis of spent catalyst show no change in crystal structure or surface species compared to fresh catalyst.
Recycling tests show conversion decreases only slightly from 99% to 97% after five cycles, with minimal Mo loss measured by ICP.

claim_summary: The activity of MoOx/nanorod-OMS-2 catalysts is dependent on the dispersion of molybdenum oxide species on the support surface, with optimal dispersion at 7.5 wt% loading.

evidence_summary: The ratio of experimental to theoretical surface density decreases with increasing Mo loading, indicating reduced dispersion at higher loadings.
Catalytic activity tests show highest conversion for Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2, correlating with maximum dispersion and active sites.

claim_summary: Small Ni particles provide active sites for H2 dissociation, and oxygen vacancies at the Ni-O-Ce interface promote CO2 activation, with an optimal ratio between these sites enhancing low-temperature CO2 methanation activity.

evidence_summary: XRD shows small Ni particles with an average size of about 6 nm for catalysts prepared by the impregnation-combustion method.
XPS confirms the presence of Ce3+ indicating oxygen vacancies, which increase with CeO2 content.

claim_summary: O2 plays a significant role in promoting SCR activity, especially in the step of NO oxidation, as evidenced by increased NOx conversion with O2 addition.

evidence_summary: NOx conversion increases sharply from 63.6% to 95.1% when O2 is introduced from 0% to 1 vol%, and further to 97.9% at 2 vol%, indicating O2's promotional effect.
